content:
# Genesis Chapter 35 — The LOGOS Bible (Formal)
**Translator:** LOGOS | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 20r–20v | **Source Images:** `source-texts/hebrew-OT/GEN_035_page_001.jpg`, `source-texts/hebrew-OT/GEN_035_page_002.jpg`
**Secondary Source:** Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), Pages 32–33 | **View LXX online:** https://digi.vatlib.it/view/MSS_Vat.gr.1209/0036

---

**1** And God said to Jacob, "Arise, go up to Bethel and dwell there. Make an altar there to God who appeared to you when you fled from your brother Esau."

**2** So Jacob said to his household and to all who were with him, "Put away the foreign gods that are among you, and purify yourselves, and change your garments.

**3** Then let us arise and go up to Bethel, so that I may make there an altar to God who answered me in the day of my distress and has been with me wherever I have gone."

**4** So they gave to Jacob all the foreign gods that were in their hands, and the rings that were in their ears, and Jacob buried them under the terebinth tree that was near Shechem.

**5** And they journeyed, and a terror from God fell upon the cities that were all around them, so that they did not pursue the sons of Jacob.

**6** So Jacob came to Luz, which is in the land of Canaan — that is, Bethel — he and all the people who were with him.

**7** And he built an altar there and called the place El-Bethel, because it was there that God had revealed himself to him when he was fleeing from his brother.

**8** And Deborah, Rebekah's nurse, died, and she was buried below Bethel under the oak. So the name of it was called Allon-bacuth.

**9** And God appeared to Jacob again when he came from Paddan-aram, and blessed him.

**10** And God said to him, "Your name is Jacob; your name shall no longer be called Jacob, but Israel shall be your name." So he called his name Israel.

**11** And God said to him, "I am El Shaddai. Be fruitful and multiply. A nation and a company of nations shall come from you, and kings shall come forth from your body.

**12** The land that I gave to Abraham and Isaac I will give to you, and I will give the land to your offspring after you."

**13** Then God went up from him in the place where he had spoken with him.

**14** And Jacob set up a pillar in the place where he had spoken with him, a pillar of stone. And he poured out a drink offering on it and poured oil on it.

**15** So Jacob called the name of the place where God had spoken with him Bethel.

**16** Then they journeyed from Bethel. And when there was still some distance to go to Ephrath, Rachel went into labor, and her labor was severe.

**17** And when her labor was at its hardest, the midwife said to her, "Do not fear, for you have another son."

**18** And as her soul was departing — for she was dying — she called his name Ben-oni. But his father called him Benjamin.

**19** So Rachel died, and she was buried on the way to Ephrath, which is Bethlehem.

**20** And Jacob set up a pillar over her tomb. That is the pillar of Rachel's tomb, which is there to this day.

**21** Then Israel journeyed on and pitched his tent beyond the tower of Eder.

**22** And while Israel was dwelling in that land, Reuben went and lay with Bilhah his father's concubine. And Israel heard of it.

Now the sons of Jacob were twelve.

**23** The sons of Leah: Reuben, Jacob's firstborn, and Simeon, Levi, Judah, Issachar, and Zebulun.

**24** The sons of Rachel: Joseph and Benjamin.

**25** The sons of Bilhah, Rachel's servant: Dan and Naphtali.

**26** The sons of Zilpah, Leah's servant: Gad and Asher. These are the sons of Jacob who were born to him in Paddan-aram.

**27** And Jacob came to his father Isaac at Mamre, at Kiriath-arba, which is Hebron, where Abraham and Isaac had sojourned.

**28** Now the days of Isaac were one hundred and eighty years.

**29** And Isaac breathed his last and died and was gathered to his people, old and full of days. And his sons Esau and Jacob buried him.

---

## LOGOS Translation Notes

### Source Manuscripts

This translation was produced from direct reading of four manuscript image sources:

1. **Leningrad Codex (Codex Leningradensis, B19A)**, dated c. 1008 CE. West Semitic Research Project photographs by Bruce E. Zuckerman. Genesis 35 spans Folios 20r–20v.

   **Folio 20r** (GEN_035_page_001.jpg): The recto of Folio 20. This page carries the conclusion of Genesis 34 in its upper portion, followed by the opening of Genesis 35 marked by a paragraph division. The three-column layout with Tiberian vocalization and cantillation marks is clearly legible throughout. The opening of Genesis 35 — God's command to Jacob to go up to Bethel (v. 1), Jacob's instruction to his household regarding foreign gods (vv. 2–3), the burial of foreign gods and earrings at Shechem (v. 4), the journey under God's terror (v. 5), the arrival at Luz-Bethel (v. 6), the building of the altar and naming El-Bethel (v. 7), Deborah's death (v. 8), God's appearance and blessing (v. 9), the re-naming as Israel (v. 10), the El Shaddai speech with fruitfulness and land promises (vv. 11–12), God's departure (v. 13), the erection of the stone pillar and libation (v. 14), the naming of Bethel (v. 15), and the departure toward Ephrath with the onset of Rachel's labor (v. 16) — are all clearly visible and legible. Masorah parva annotations appear in the inter-column margins.

   **Folio 20v** (GEN_035_page_002.jpg): The verso of Folio 20. This page carries the completion of Genesis 35: the midwife's assurance (v. 17), Rachel's dying naming of Ben-oni and Jacob's renaming as Benjamin (v. 18), Rachel's death and burial at Ephrath-Bethlehem (v. 19), the pillar over Rachel's tomb (v. 20), Israel's journey to the tower of Eder (v. 21), Reuben's sin with Bilhah and the terse note "and Israel heard" (v. 22), the enumeration formula "the sons of Jacob were twelve" (v. 22b), the list of all twelve sons by their mothers (vv. 23–26), Jacob's arrival at his father Isaac at Mamre-Hebron (v. 27), Isaac's age of 180 years (v. 28), and Isaac's death with the standard patriarchal burial formula (v. 29). The page then continues into the beginning of Genesis 36. The text is legible throughout with consistent Masoretic pointing.

2. **Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209)** — Septuagint/LXX, 4th century CE. Two pages were read directly.

   **Page 32** (GEN_035_vaticanus_page_001.jpg): Vaticanus page 32 (digi.vatlib.it page pa_0036). The page heading "Γεν ΧΧΧIV" is visible at the top center. This page carries the conclusion of Genesis 34 and continues into Genesis 35. The continuous uncial Greek text in three columns is legible. The LXX text of Genesis 35 begins partway through this page, carrying the divine command to Jacob, the household purification, the journey, the terror upon surrounding cities, the arrival at Bethel, El-Bethel, Deborah's death, the divine re-appearance and blessing, the name confirmation, and the El Shaddai speech.

   **Page 33** (GEN_035_vaticanus_page_002.jpg): Vaticanus page 33 (digi.vatlib.it page pa_0037). The page heading "Γεν ΧΛ" (indicating chapter 35 / 36 area) is visible at top center. This page carries the continuation of Genesis 35: Rachel's labor and death, the tomb pillar, Reuben's sin, the list of twelve sons, Isaac's death — and then the beginning of Genesis 36. The text is legible throughout.

---

### Key Translation Decisions

**1. "Put away the foreign gods" — הָסִרוּ אֶת־אֱלֹהֵי הַנֵּכָר (v. 2)**

The verb הָסִרוּ (hasiru — imperative plural of סוּר, sur — "to turn aside, to remove, to put away") combined with אֱלֹהֵי הַנֵּכָר (elohei ha-nekhar — "gods of the foreign / the alien gods") constitutes a formal command for cultic purification. This is the same combination of foreignness and divine otherness that the Hebrew Bible consistently treats as incompatible with covenant loyalty. The text does not explain where these foreign gods came from, but the narrative context strongly implies the aftermath of the Shechem episode (chapter 34) — the household included those who had been acquired in or through the interaction with Shechem, and possibly pre-existing teraphim. The formal translation renders "Put away the foreign gods that are among you" — preserving the imperative force and the phrase's theological edge. The instruction to "purify yourselves" (וְהִטַּהֲרוּ, vehi-taharu — "cleanse/purify yourselves") and "change your garments" (וְהַחֲלִיפוּ שִׂמְלֹתֵיכֶם, veha-chalifu simlotekem) are standard pre-theophany ritual requirements (compare Exodus 19:10, 14). The LXX renders ἀφέλετε τοὺς θεοὺς τοὺς ἀλλοτρίους (aphelete tous theous tous allotriōus — "remove the foreign gods"), consistent with the MT.

**2. "The rings that were in their ears" — הַנְּזָמִים אֲשֶׁר בְּאָזְנֵיהֶם (v. 4)**

Jacob buries not only the foreign gods (teraphim, idols) but also the earrings (נְזָמִים, nezamim) that his household possesses. The significance of the earrings is debated: they may have served as amulets or votive ornaments associated with pagan cults rather than mere jewelry, a reading supported by the parallel in Exodus 32:2–3 where Aaron collects gold earrings to cast the golden calf. The fact that Jacob buries them alongside the gods suggests a cultic or apotropaic function. The formal translation renders "the rings that were in their ears" — preserving the Hebrew's identification of their location (ears) without over-interpreting their function. The LXX renders τὰ ἐνώτια τὰ ἐν τοῖς ὠσὶν αὐτῶν (ta ēnōtia ta en tois ōsin autōn — "the earrings that were in their ears"), consistent with the MT.

**3. "A terror from God" — חִתַּת אֱלֹהִים (v. 5)**

The phrase חִתַּת אֱלֹהִים (chittat Elohim — literally "a dread/terror of God") describes the fear that fell on the surrounding cities preventing them from pursuing Jacob after the Shechem massacre. The noun חִתִּית (chittit — "terror, dread") is from the root חָתַת (chatat — "to be shattered, to be dismayed"). The genitive "of God" (אֱלֹהִים) functions as a superlative or intensifying genitive — an overwhelming, God-sent terror — or as an attributive identifying the terror's divine source. The formal translation renders "a terror from God fell upon the cities," treating the genitive as a divine-source genitive (terror sent from God) rather than a pure superlative, which is theologically consistent with the narrative context of divine protection of Jacob's journey. The LXX renders ἔκστασις θεοῦ (ekstasis theou — "an ecstasy/terror of God"), consistent with the MT's sense.

**4. "El-Bethel" — אֵל בֵּית־אֵל (v. 7)**

The name אֵל בֵּית־אֵל (El Bethel — "God of Bethel" / "God, House of God") is preserved untranslated as a proper divine epithet, following the practice throughout the LOGOS Bible of retaining compound divine names in transliteration (compare El Shaddai, El Roi, El Elyon). The place is named for the divine encounter rather than merely for the location. The LXX renders Βαιθηλ (Baithēl) here, in the context of naming the altar rather than giving the full epithet El-Bethel; this is a minor divergence noted in the table below.

**5. "Allon-bacuth" — אַלּוֹן בָּכוּת (v. 8)**

The place-name אַלּוֹן בָּכוּת (Allon-bacuth — "Oak of Weeping") is preserved in transliteration following the standard formal equivalent practice of retaining proper nouns. The death of Deborah, Rebekah's nurse, is noted with no elaboration. This brief notice is one of the most quietly affecting in the patriarchal narratives: a woman who appears only here and in her introduction (Genesis 24:59 — unnamed "her nurse"), whose decades of life in the household of Rebekah and then Jacob are summed in a single verse, is mourned with a name that the land itself remembers. The formal translation retains "Allon-bacuth" as the place name. The LXX renders βαλανος πενθους (balanos penthous — "oak of mourning/grief"), a Greek rendering of the meaning rather than a transliteration; the formal translation follows the MT transliteration.

**6. The Second Naming — Israel (v. 10)**

This is the second divine conferral of the name Israel on Jacob. The first occurred at the Jabbok (Genesis 32:28), in the context of Jacob's nocturnal wrestling. Here, at Bethel, God restates the name in a formal theophanic address: "Your name is Jacob; your name shall no longer be called Jacob, but Israel shall be your name." The formal translation preserves the dual formulation — first the acknowledgment of the current name, then the authoritative replacement — which is present in the MT (לֹא-יִקָּרֵא שִׁמְךָ עוֹד יַעֲקֹב, "your name shall no longer be called Jacob") and gives the renaming the weight of a formal covenant act. The LXX renders οὐ κληθήσεται τὸ ὄνομά σου Ιακωβ (ou klēthēsetai to onoma sou Iakōb — "your name shall not be called Jacob"), consistent with the MT.

**7. "El Shaddai" — אֵל שַׁדַּי (v. 11)**

The divine name אֵל שַׁדַּי (El Shaddai) is preserved untranslated. The name was introduced to Abraham in Genesis 17:1 and is one of the central patriarchal epithets for God. Its precise etymology remains disputed (possible derivations: "God of the Mountain," "God Almighty," "God of the Breast/Nurture"), but its usage in the Pentateuch consistently marks moments of covenantal promise regarding progeny and land. The formal translation retains "El Shaddai" without rendering it as "God Almighty" (the traditional LXX and KJV equivalent), preserving the distinct voice of the patriarchal divine-name tradition. The LXX renders ἐγώ εἰμι θεός σου (egō eimi theos sou — "I am your God"), replacing the Hebrew name-title with a more generic identification — a significant divergence noted in the variant table below.

**8. "A nation and a company of nations" — גּוֹי וּקְהַל גּוֹיִם (v. 11)**

The phrase גּוֹי וּקְהַל גּוֹיִם (goy uqehal goyim — "a nation and a company/assembly of nations") echoes the promise to Abraham (Genesis 17:4–6: "father of a multitude of nations" / "father of many nations") but with the additional term קָהָל (qahal — "assembly, congregation, company"). The word qahal is the same term used for Israel's assembly before God (Deuteronomy 23:2–8; Numbers 20:4), suggesting that what is promised here is not merely ethnic proliferation but a covenantal community of peoples. The formal translation renders "a nation and a company of nations" — preserving the qahal term's community/assembly resonance. The LXX renders ἔθνη καὶ συναγωγαὶ ἐθνῶν (ethnē kai synagōgai ethnōn — "nations and assemblies of nations"), consistent with the MT and adding the plural "assemblies."

**9. "A pillar of stone" and the Drink Offering — מַצֵּבַת אָבֶן / נֶסֶךְ (vv. 14)**

Jacob's erection of a מַצֵּבָה (massebah — "standing stone, pillar, stele") at the site of the theophany is a repetition of his earlier act at Bethel (Genesis 28:18, 22 — the same stone that he had set up as a pillar and anointed). Here the act is elaborated: he pours a נֶסֶךְ (nesek — "drink offering / libation") on it and then anoints it with oil (שֶׁמֶן, shemen). The drink offering is the same term used for the Mosaic cultic libations (Exodus 29:40, Numbers 15:5–7), giving Jacob's act a proto-cultic significance — the patriarchal practice anticipates the later Levitical liturgy. The formal translation renders "a pillar of stone" (preserving both components of the Hebrew, matsevah + even) and "poured out a drink offering" (preserving nesek). The LXX renders στήλην λιθίνην (stēlēn lithinēn — "a stone stele") and σπονδήν (spondēn — "a libation"), consistent with the MT.

**10. "Kivrat-ha'arets" — כִּבְרַת הָאָרֶץ (v. 16)**

The phrase כִּבְרַת הָאָרֶץ (kivrat-ha'arets) is a hapax legomenon — appearing in biblical Hebrew only here and in verses 19 and 48:7 (the latter recalling this same incident). Its meaning is uncertain: it seems to denote a unit of distance or area ("a stretch of land," "a league," "some distance"), but no precise equivalent is established. The formal translation renders "some distance to go to Ephrath" — following the most widely accepted interpretation (an indefinite but modest distance) — rather than attempting a speculative exact measurement. The LXX renders ἱππόδρομος γῆς (hippodromos gēs — "a horse-course / hippodrome of land"), suggesting it understood the term as a specific if somewhat large unit of linear distance. The formal translation chooses clarity over literalism at this point of genuine textual obscurity.

**11. "Ben-oni" and "Benjamin" — בֶּן־אוֹנִי / בִּנְיָמִין (v. 18)**

The dying Rachel names her son בֶּן־אוֹנִי (Ben-oni). The term אוֹן (on) is ambiguous: it can mean "sorrow, grief, mourning" (yielding "son of my sorrow") or "strength, power, vigor" (yielding "son of my strength" — in the sense of the vital energy expended in the birth). The ambiguity may be intentional: the name captures both dimensions of the birth-death moment. Jacob's counter-naming, בִּנְיָמִין (Binyamin — Benjamin), is also capable of more than one reading: "son of the right hand" (yamin — right hand, the side of strength and favor) or "son of the south" (yamin — south, since the tribe of Benjamin was to occupy the southernmost territory of the northern tribes). The formal translation preserves both names without interpretation: "she called his name Ben-oni" and "his father called him Benjamin." The tension between the mother's name (death, sorrow) and the father's renaming (life, favor) is left for the reader to hold. The LXX renders Υἱὸς ὀδύνης μου (Huios odynēs mou — "son of my pain/grief") for Ben-oni and Βενιαμιν (Beniamin) for Benjamin.

**12. "As her soul was departing" — בְּצֵאת נַפְשָׁהּ (v. 18)**

The phrase בְּצֵאת נַפְשָׁהּ (be-tset nafshalh — "in the going out of her soul") is one of the most poignant expressions of death in the Hebrew Bible. The verb יָצָא (yatsa — "to go out, to depart, to come forth") is the same verb used of birth — a child "comes forth" from the womb — so that Rachel's death is described with the same verb as the birth she is in the act of accomplishing. The soul "goes out" as the child "comes out." The formal translation renders "as her soul was departing — for she was dying" — preserving the present-continuous force of the participial phrase and supplying the narrator's explanatory gloss (כִּי מֵתָה, ki metah — "for she was dying") in an em-dash construction that marks it as an authorial intrusion rather than a clause of equal weight. The LXX renders ἐν τῷ ἀφιέναι αὐτὴν τὴν ψυχήν (en tō aphienai autēn tēn psychēn — "as she was releasing her soul"), consistent with the MT.

**13. Reuben's Sin — "lay with Bilhah his father's concubine" (v. 22)**

The notice of Reuben's sin with Bilhah is given with deliberate narrative compression: a single clause followed by silence. The text reports: "Reuben went and lay with Bilhah his father's concubine. And Israel heard of it." Then nothing. No divine judgment, no paternal confrontation, no narrative consequence — in this chapter. The consequence is deferred to Genesis 49:3–4, where the dying Jacob strips Reuben of the firstborn's preeminence for this act: "you went up to your father's bed; then you defiled it." The formal translation renders the verse as two clauses with the stark pause preserved: the sin stated, the hearing stated, then the paragraph break that is itself part of the text's meaning — the silence of Israel (= the silence of the nation in embryo) confronted with this violation. The Hebrew "his father's concubine" (פִּילֶגֶשׁ אָבִיו, pilegesh aviv) is rendered directly; the term פִּילֶגֶשׁ (pilegesh — concubine, a secondary wife of legally recognized but lower-status position) is rendered "concubine" following established English convention. The LXX renders παλλακὴν τοῦ πατρὸς αὐτοῦ (pallakēn tou patros autou — "the concubine of his father"), consistent with the MT.

**14. The Twelve Sons List (vv. 22b–26)**

The enumeration of the twelve sons by their four mothers (Leah, Rachel, Bilhah, Zilpah) follows the conventional genealogical formula of the patriarchal narratives. The phrase "who were born to him in Paddan-aram" at the close of verse 26 is technically inaccurate for Benjamin, who was just born in Canaan — but the formula may refer to the household composition as it stood during the Paddan-aram years, with Benjamin here implicitly excepted, or may represent a traditional list with slightly imprecise framing. The formal translation follows the MT text directly without harmonization. The LXX is consistent with the MT in the list.

**15. "Gathered to his people" — וַיֵּאָסֶף אֶל־עַמָּיו (v. 29)**

The phrase וַיֵּאָסֶף אֶל־עַמָּיו (vayyiassef el-ammav — "and he was gathered to his people") is the standard biblical idiom for death understood as reunion with one's ancestors — not merely a physical description of burial but a formulation that implies an ongoing community of the dead. It is used consistently of the patriarchs (Abraham in Genesis 25:8, Ishmael in 25:17, Isaac here, Jacob in 49:29, 33, Aaron in Numbers 20:24, Moses in Numbers 27:13). The phrase is distinct from burial ("and his sons buried him") — gathering to one's people precedes or accompanies the act of burial and refers to something other than the physical interment. The formal translation renders "was gathered to his people" — the established English equivalent for this idiom — followed by "old and full of days" (זָקֵן וּשְׂבַע יָמִים, zaqen usvea' yamim — "old and satisfied/satiated with days"), the same formula used of Abraham's death (Genesis 25:8). The LXX renders προσετέθη πρὸς τὸ γένος αὐτοῦ (prosetethē pros to genos autou — "was added to his race/kin"), consistent with the MT's sense.

---

### LXX (Vaticanus) Variants — Genesis 35

Pages 32–33 of Codex Vaticanus were read directly. The continuous uncial Greek text was legible throughout. Key variants observed:

| Verse (MT) | MT (Leningrad Codex) | LXX (Vaticanus) | Translation Decision | Note |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 35:1 | אֲשֶׁר נִרְאָה אֵלֶיךָ ("who appeared to you") | ὁ ὀφθείς σοι ("who appeared to you") | "who appeared to you" | MT and LXX consistent |
| 35:4 | תַּחַת הָאֵלָה ("under the terebinth/oak") | ὑπὸ τὴν τερέβινθον ("under the terebinth") | "under the terebinth tree" | LXX uses τερέβινθος (terebinth), consistent with MT's אֵלָה |
| 35:7 | וַיִּקְרָא לַמָּקוֹם אֵל בֵּית־אֵל ("he called the place El-Bethel") | καὶ ἐκάλεσεν τὸ ὄνομα τοῦ τόπου Βαιθηλ ("he called the name of the place Bethel") | "El-Bethel" (MT) | LXX omits the "El" prefix, reducing to simply "Bethel"; MT preserves the compound divine name; formal translation follows MT |
| 35:8 | אַלּוֹן בָּכוּת ("Allon-bacuth" — Oak of Weeping) | βαλανος πενθους ("oak of mourning") | "Allon-bacuth" | LXX translates the meaning rather than transliterating; formal translation retains the Hebrew place-name per LOGOS practice |
| 35:11 | אֵל שַׁדַּי ("El Shaddai") | ἐγώ εἰμι θεός σου ("I am your God") | "El Shaddai" (MT) | LXX replaces the divine name-title with a generic address, losing the specific patriarchal epithet; formal translation preserves El Shaddai as a proper divine name |
| 35:11 | גּוֹי וּקְהַל גּוֹיִם ("a nation and a company of nations") | ἔθνη καὶ συναγωγαὶ ἐθνῶν ("nations and assemblies of nations") | "a nation and a company of nations" | LXX pluralizes both nouns; MT's singular "a nation" (goy) followed by "a company of nations" (qehal goyim) is a more structured formulation; formal translation follows MT |
| 35:14 | וַיַּסֵּךְ עָלֶיהָ נֶסֶךְ ("he poured out a drink offering on it") | καὶ ἔσπεισεν ἐπ᾽ αὐτῆς σπονδήν ("he poured a libation on it") | "poured out a drink offering" | Consistent; both attest the libation |
| 35:16 | כִּבְרַת הָאָרֶץ ("a kivrat of land") | ἱππόδρομος γῆς ("a horse-course of land") | "some distance" | LXX interprets the obscure term as a specific distance measurement (a horse-track's length); MT's term remains uncertain; formal translation uses "some distance" for clarity |
| 35:18 | בֶּן־אוֹנִי ("Ben-oni") | Υἱὸς ὀδύνης μου ("son of my pain") | "Ben-oni" (transliterated) | LXX translates "Ben-oni" as "son of my pain," choosing one of the two possible meanings (sorrow/pain rather than strength); formal translation preserves the Hebrew name, allowing both readings |
| 35:22 | וַיִּשְׁמַע יִשְׂרָאֵל ("and Israel heard") | ἤκουσεν δὲ Ισραηλ ("and Israel heard") | "And Israel heard of it" | Consistent in both; both preserve the stark silence after the hearing |
| 35:26 | אֲשֶׁר יֻלַּד־לוֹ בְּפַדַּן אֲרָם ("who were born to him in Paddan-aram") | οἳ ἐγεννήθησαν αὐτῷ ἐν Μεσοποταμίᾳ ("who were born to him in Mesopotamia") | "born to him in Paddan-aram" | LXX uses Μεσοποταμία (Mesopotamia) as the equivalent for Paddan-aram; consistent in meaning |
| 35:29 | וַיֵּאָסֶף אֶל־עַמָּיו ("gathered to his people") | προσετέθη πρὸς τὸ γένος αὐτοῦ ("added to his kin/race") | "gathered to his people" | Both attest the concept of post-mortem reunion with one's ancestors; formal translation follows the well-established English rendering |

---

### Structural and Theological Notes

**The Architecture of Genesis 35 — Return, Confirmation, and Loss**

Genesis 35 functions as a theological capstone to the Jacob narrative that began at Bethel in Genesis 28. The chapter's movement is triadic:

- **Return to Bethel (vv. 1–15):** God commands Jacob back to the place of origin, the site of the first theophany (Genesis 28:10–22). Before the return, the household undergoes purification — the foreign gods buried, the cultic earrings interred. The journey is made under divine protection (the terror-of-God, v. 5). At Bethel, the theophany is renewed: the name Israel is formally confirmed (for the second time, cf. 32:28), El Shaddai is identified, the covenantal promises of progeny and land are restated in the fullest patriarchal form, and Jacob sets up a stone pillar with libation and anointing — completing the vow he made at Bethel in 28:20–22. The circle is closed.

- **Death on the Road (vv. 16–22):** Between Bethel and Ephrath, Rachel dies in childbirth. The juxtaposition is stark: the life-promise of El Shaddai ("be fruitful and multiply," v. 11) is enacted at the cost of the mother's life. Benjamin, the last of the twelve, is born as Rachel departs. Her two names for the child — Ben-oni (the dying mother's name) and Benjamin (the living father's name) — stand as a permanently embedded memorial to the cost of that fruitfulness. The chapter then records, in a single breathless sentence, Reuben's desecration of his father's bed with Bilhah — an act of violation within the household immediately after the household's greatest external loss. Jacob-Israel hears and is silent.

- **Death at the Destination (vv. 27–29):** Jacob reaches Isaac at Hebron. Isaac dies at 180 years, old and full of days, gathered to his people, buried by Esau and Jacob together. The reconciliation implied by the joint burial (compare Genesis 25:9, where Ishmael and Isaac together bury Abraham) stands without comment.

**El Shaddai and the Covenant Form of v. 11–12**

The speech of El Shaddai in verses 11–12 is one of the most compressed and complete restatements of the Abrahamic covenant in the Pentateuch. It contains four elements: (1) the divine self-identification by name/title ("I am El Shaddai"); (2) the procreative command ("be fruitful and multiply"); (3) the progeny promise with political specification ("a nation and a company of nations... kings shall come forth from your body"); (4) the land promise with both the prior grant to Abraham and Isaac and the forward grant to Jacob's offspring. This four-element form is a deliberate theological summary, connecting the Jacob narrative to the Abrahamic promise and projecting it forward to the Mosaic and monarchic periods. "Kings shall come forth from your body" is particularly significant: it grounds the Davidic kingship in covenantal promise as early as the patriarchal period.

**The Silence of Israel (v. 22)**

The half-verse "And Israel heard of it" — followed by the paragraph break and the enumeration formula — is one of the most studied silences in the Hebrew Bible. There is no recorded response. No confrontation, no grief, no prayer, no divine word. The silence may be read in multiple ways: as a father's overwhelming shame, as the narrator's refusal to adjudicate between Jacob and Reuben, as a deliberate withholding of the confrontation that will come only at the deathbed (Genesis 49:3–4). The formal translation preserves the silence precisely as the text presents it — statement, then break, then new subject.

**The Dual Burial at Hebron (v. 29)**

"And his sons Esau and Jacob buried him" echoes the burial of Abraham by "his sons Isaac and Ishmael" (Genesis 25:9). The pattern of fraternal reunion in death — brothers who have been divided, deceived, and estranged coming together to honor the father's body — is one of the recurring structural elegies of Genesis. The formal translation follows the Hebrew word order: Esau named before Jacob, as befits the older son, even in this chapter whose narrative is about Jacob.
